metadata.description: Triage KB: Symptom → Care Setting routing based on ACEP ESI algorithm and CMS coverage rules
sources: CMS Medicare Benefit Policy Manual Ch.6 (2024)
metadata.disclaimer: This KB is for care navigation only. Never diagnoses. Medical advisor review required before deployment.

entries.symptom: chest pain radiating to left arm with diaphoresis

alternative_settings.note: Only if pain is clearly musculoskeletal, <30 years old, no cardiac risk factors, and fully stable — extremely rare exception

entries.reasoning: Classic ACS presentation. Radiating chest pain + diaphoresis = high-probability STEMI/NSTEMI until proven otherwise. AHA/ACC guidelines mandate 12-lead ECG within 10 minutes of arrival. Do not delay for any triage evaluation.

insurance_notes.medicare: Emergency services covered at 80% after Part B deductible ($240 in 2024). No prior auth required for emergencies. Prudent layperson standard applies.
insurance_notes.uhc: Emergency services covered per EOB. No prior auth required. Prudent layperson standard — coverage based on presenting symptoms, not final diagnosis.
insurance_notes.general: Ambulance (911) transport to nearest appropriate ER is covered under most plans without prior auth.

primary.document: AHA/ACC 2023 Guideline for Diagnosis and Management of Acute Coronary Syndromes

entries.symptom: stroke symptoms: sudden face drooping, arm weakness, speech difficulty
entries.time_to_treatment: immediate — tPA window is 4.5 hours from symptom onset
entries.reasoning: Every minute of stroke = 1.9 million neurons lost. tPA (tissue plasminogen activator) must be given within 4.5 hours of symptom onset. Mechanical thrombectomy window is up to 24 hours in selected patients. Time to treatment is the single most important determinant of outcome.

insurance_notes.medicare: Inpatient stroke care covered under Part A. tPA covered as inpatient drug.
insurance_notes.uhc: Emergency and inpatient stroke care covered. No prior auth for emergencies.

primary.document: AHA/ASA 2019 Guidelines for Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke

entries.symptom: anaphylaxis: hives + throat swelling + difficulty breathing after exposure to allergen
entries.reasoning: Anaphylaxis is rapidly fatal without epinephrine. If epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) used, ER visit still mandatory — biphasic reactions occur in up to 20% of cases, requiring observation for 4–6 hours minimum.

insurance_notes.medicare: Emergency services covered. Epinephrine administered in ER covered under Part B.
insurance_notes.uhc: Emergency covered. No prior auth required.

primary.document: ACAAI/AAAAI Anaphylaxis Practice Parameter Update 2023

entries.symptom: severe shortness of breath at rest

alternative_settings.note: Only if mild dyspnea on exertion (not rest), no cyanosis, SpO2 ≥94%, known cause (e.g. controlled asthma mild flare)

insurance_notes.medicare: Emergency and observation covered. Oxygen therapy covered under DME (Part B) for home use if qualifying criteria met.
insurance_notes.uhc: Emergency covered without prior auth. Inpatient may require notification within 24–48 hours.

primary.document: ACEP Emergency Medicine Clinical Policies: Dyspnea

entries.symptom: thunderclap headache: sudden severe headache described as worst of life
entries.reasoning: Thunderclap headache is subarachnoid hemorrhage until proven otherwise. SAH carries 40–50% mortality; of survivors, 30% have permanent disability. CT without contrast has 98% sensitivity within 6 hours of onset; LP required if CT negative.

insurance_notes.medicare: Emergency evaluation covered. CT and LP covered under Part B in ER setting.

primary.document: ACEP Clinical Policy: Headache (2019, reaffirmed 2023)

entries.symptom: high fever with stiff neck and sensitivity to light
entries.reasoning: Classic meningitis triad (fever + nuchal rigidity + photophobia). Bacterial meningitis has 20–30% mortality without treatment; antibiotics must be started within 1 hour of presentation. Do not wait for CT before LP if no focal neuro deficits.

insurance_notes.medicare: Emergency + inpatient covered under Parts A and B.
insurance_notes.uhc: Emergency covered without prior auth. Inpatient notification within 24 hours required.

primary.document: IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines: Bacterial Meningitis (2004, reaffirmed per current evidence 2022)

entries.symptom: uncontrolled bleeding not stopping with direct pressure after 10 minutes

alternative_settings.note: If bleeding controlled with pressure, wound is <4cm, no arterial involvement, and urgent care has suture capability

insurance_notes.medicare: Both emergency and urgent care covered. Urgent care under Part B.
insurance_notes.uhc: Both settings covered. Urgent care preferred for minor lacerations if clinically appropriate.

primary.document: ACEP Clinical Policy: Wound Management

entries.symptom: suspected broken bone or fracture after injury

alternative_settings.note: Required for: open fractures, suspected femur/pelvis/spine fracture, neurovascular compromise, or significant trauma mechanism
Hairline fractures with confirmed imaging at urgent care can follow up with PCP

insurance_notes.medicare: X-ray covered under Part B in urgent care. Splinting/casting covered. Orthopedic surgery covered under Part A if inpatient.
insurance_notes.uhc: Urgent care and ER both covered. Urgent care preferred for stable extremity fractures.

primary.document: ACEP Clinical Policy: Fracture Management

entries.symptom: urinary tract infection symptoms: burning urination, frequent urination, no fever

alternative_settings.note: If telehealth not available or symptoms severe
Next available appointment appropriate if symptoms are mild
Only if fever >101°F + flank pain (kidney infection/sepsis risk) or pregnant

insurance_notes.medicare: Telehealth covered under Part B (expanded post-COVID permanently for some services). Urine culture covered when clinically indicated.
insurance_notes.uhc: Telehealth covered via UHC Virtual Visit. $0 cost for many plans for virtual UTI visit.

primary.document: IDSA Guidelines: Uncomplicated UTI in Women (2011, reaffirmed 2022)

entries.symptom: upper respiratory infection: sore throat, runny nose, mild cough, no high fever

alternative_settings.note: If patient wants strep test, antiviral for flu, or has questions about symptoms
If strep suspected (fever >101°F + white exudate + no cough) or flu with high-risk factors

entries.self_care_instructions: Rest, hydration (8+ glasses water/day), OTC symptom relief (acetaminophen for pain/fever, saline nasal rinse, honey for cough in adults). Expect 7–10 days duration. Antibiotics do NOT treat viral URI — prescribing them for viral URI is inappropriate.

escalate_to_telehealth_if: severe throat pain with difficulty swallowing

emergency.member_liability: High copay — NOT recommended for URI

insurance_notes.medicare: OTC medications not covered. Telehealth covered under expanded Part B.
insurance_notes.uhc: Telehealth/virtual visits recommended. Many plans $0 copay for virtual URI visit.

primary.document: CDC Common Cold: Treatment and Management

secondary.document: AAFP Clinical Practice Guideline: Acute Rhinosinusitis (2015)

entries.symptom: abdominal pain severe or with rigid abdomen

alternative_settings.note: Only if pain is mild, chronic, no peritoneal signs, and no high-risk features

insurance_notes.medicare: Abdominal CT covered when medically necessary under Part B in ER. Surgical intervention under Part A.
insurance_notes.uhc: Emergency covered. CT imaging requires coverage under ER benefit.

primary.document: ACEP Clinical Policy: Evaluation of Acute Abdominal Pain

entries.symptom: mental health crisis: expressing thoughts of self-harm or suicide

alternative_settings.note: If active plan, means access, or imminent danger — call 911

crisis_resources.primary: Call or text 988 (Suicide & Crisis Lifeline) — 24/7, free, confidential
crisis_resources.secondary: Crisis Text Line: Text HOME to 741741

do_not: promise confidentiality if safety at risk

insurance_notes.medicare: Mental health emergency covered. Inpatient psychiatric covered under Part A. 190-day Medicare lifetime limit for psychiatric hospital (not general hospital psych unit).
insurance_notes.uhc: Mental health emergency covered under Mental Health Parity Act. Behavioral health benefits equal to medical.

primary.document: SAMHSA 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline Implementation

secondary.document: Joint Commission National Patient Safety Goal NPSG.15.01.01 (Suicide Risk Reduction)

entries.symptom: acute low back pain after lifting, no neurological symptoms
symptom_aliases: lower back pain no leg symptoms

alternative_settings.note: For medication consultation (NSAIDs, muscle relaxants) or work note
If not improving after 1–2 weeks of self-care

entries.self_care_instructions: Ice first 48 hours, then heat. NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) superior to acetaminophen for acute back pain. Continue light activity (bed rest is harmful). 90% of acute low back pain resolves within 6 weeks.

emergency.member_liability: High — imaging often not indicated acutely

insurance_notes.medicare: Physical therapy covered under Part B (must be medically necessary). X-ray/MRI covered if red flags present. Chiropractic covered for manipulation only.
insurance_notes.uhc: Physical therapy covered with referral. MRI requires prior auth if ordered in outpatient setting without red flags.

primary.document: ACP Clinical Practice Guideline: Noninvasive Treatments for Low Back Pain (2017)

entries.symptom: diabetic with blood glucose > 400 mg/dL

alternative_settings.note: If glucose 300-400, asymptomatic, Type 2 DM, able to keep fluids down, no ketones — call PCP/Telehealth first
If glucose mildly elevated 200-300, no symptoms, Type 2 DM on oral meds — consult for medication adjustment

insurance_notes.medicare: DKA inpatient covered Part A. CGM covered under Part B (medically necessary criteria).
insurance_notes.uhc: Emergency DKA covered. CGM covered with prior auth for qualifying Type 1 and some Type 2 patients.

primary.document: ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes 2024 — Section 16: Diabetes Care in the Hospital

entries.symptom: pediatric fever: child under 3 months with any fever
entries.reasoning: Fever ≥100.4°F (38°C) in infant <3 months requires full sepsis workup (CBC, UA, blood culture, LP) regardless of appearance. Neonates cannot localize infection — what appears 'mild' can be bacterial sepsis. Rochester/Philadelphia/Boston criteria guide workup stratification.

insurance_notes.medicare: Generally not applicable for neonates (Medicare is primarily age 65+).
insurance_notes.uhc: Pediatric emergency covered. CHIP/Medicaid for eligible children.
insurance_notes.general: Check plan for pediatric emergency copay — often lower than adult ER copay.

primary.document: AAP Clinical Practice Guideline: Evaluation and Management of Febrile Infants 8-60 Days

entries.symptom: hypertensive urgency: blood pressure reading > 180/120 mmHg, no symptoms
symptom_aliases: very high blood pressure no symptoms

alternative_settings.note: REQUIRED if ANY symptoms: chest pain, shortness of breath, headache, vision changes, altered consciousness, or focal neuro deficits (hypertensive emergency)
If BP is slightly elevated (160-180/100-110), asymptomatic, known hypertensive patient on medications — consult for med adjustment

severity_modifiers.note: Hypertensive urgency (no symptoms) vs hypertensive emergency (symptoms + end organ damage) is a critical distinction

insurance_notes.medicare: Anti-hypertensives covered under Part D. Blood pressure monitoring device: not typically covered. ABPM covered for white coat hypertension diagnosis.
insurance_notes.uhc: 24-hour ABPM covered with prior auth for specific indications. Telehealth for HTN management covered.

primary.document: AHA/ACC 2017 High Blood Pressure Clinical Practice Guideline

entries.symptom: COPD exacerbation: worsening shortness of breath, increased sputum

alternative_settings.note: If SpO2 <90%, accessory muscle use, altered mental status, unable to speak full sentences, or severe distress
Mild exacerbation only — worse than usual but able to function, SpO2 ≥94%, for prednisone/antibiotic prescription

primary.document: GOLD 2024 Global Strategy for COPD

entries.symptom: asthma attack: wheezing, coughing, shortness of breath not responding to rescue inhaler

alternative_settings.note: Mild-moderate attack with partial response to bronchodilator, SpO2 ≥94%, able to speak in sentences — if UC has nebulizer capability

primary.document: NAEPP Expert Panel Report 3: Guidelines for Asthma Management (2007, updated 2020)

entries.symptom: possible pulmonary embolism: sudden onset pleuritic chest pain with shortness of breath
symptom_aliases: sharp chest pain worse with breathing
calf pain swelling with chest pain
entries.reasoning: Wells PE Score: if intermediate or high probability, d-dimer or CT-PA in ER. Massive PE causes hemodynamic instability and death without immediate anticoagulation or thrombolysis.

primary.document: ACEP Clinical Policy: Thromboembolic Disease (2018)

entries.symptom: eye injury: chemical splash to eye
entries.immediate_action: Irrigate immediately with water for 20+ minutes BEFORE transport. Do not wait. Every second of irrigation before ER reduces tissue damage.

primary.document: AAO Clinical Practice Guidelines: Ocular Chemical Burns

entries.symptom: mild ankle sprain after sports injury, able to bear weight

alternative_settings.note: If Ottawa Ankle Rules positive (bone tenderness at specific points + inability to bear weight 4 steps)

entries.self_care_instructions: RICE: Rest, Ice (20min on/20min off), Compression (ACE wrap), Elevation. NSAIDs for pain/swelling. Ottawa Ankle Rules: if tenderness at posterior malleolus or base of 5th metatarsal AND cannot bear weight, X-ray needed.

primary.document: Ottawa Ankle Rules (Stiell et al.)

entries.symptom: COVID-19 symptoms: fever, cough, loss of taste/smell, fatigue

alternative_settings.note: If mild symptoms, vaccinated, low risk — home isolation, OTC symptom management
For Paxlovid prescription eligibility (high-risk patients, within 5 days of symptom onset)
If SpO2 <94%, persistent chest pain, confusion, or inability to stay awake

primary.document: NIH COVID-19 Treatment Guidelines (2024)

entries.symptom: heart failure patient: sudden weight gain 3+ lbs in 24 hours, ankle swelling, shortness of breath

alternative_settings.note: Mild worsening: 1-2 lb weight gain, mild ankle edema, no dyspnea — phone/telehealth with PCP for diuretic adjustment

primary.document: AHA/ACC 2022 Heart Failure Guideline

entries.symptom: migraine with typical aura in known migraineur

alternative_settings.note: For triptan prescription refill, anti-emetic, or guidance if OTC not effective
For IV fluids + IV metoclopramide + ketorolac if oral medications not working

entries.note: Only applies to KNOWN migraineur with TYPICAL migraine presentation. First/worst headache → TRIAGE_005 (thunderclap → EMERGENCY).

primary.document: AAN Clinical Practice Guideline: Migraine Treatment (2012, updated 2019)

entries.symptom: prescription medication refill needed, no urgent symptoms

alternative_settings.note: Next available appointment; some medications require in-person evaluation for refill

entries.note: Controlled substances (Schedule II: opioids, stimulants) require in-person evaluation in most states. Electronic prescribing of controlled substances (EPCS) laws vary. Telehealth cannot prescribe Schedule II in most states.

emergency.member_liability: HIGH — ER not appropriate for non-urgent refills

insurance_notes.medicare: Part D covers most outpatient medications. Telehealth prescribing under waiver rules post-public health emergency.
insurance_notes.uhc: Telehealth prescribing covered for non-controlled medications. Mail-order pharmacy available for 90-day supply.

primary.document: DEA Telehealth Prescribing Rules (21 CFR Part 1306)
